Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place, but I tried to make it work. I'd dedicate around 30 hours a week, usually in chunks between my extracurricular activities and part-time commitments. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes that helped me process the material. Some weeks I was incredibly organized with specific blocks of time, and other weeks I was just scrambling to keep up with everything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. When deadlines were approaching, I'd go into overdrive and study way more intensely. But during quieter weeks, I might spread things out more evenly. The anxiety of having multiple things due at once often pushed me to study at irregular hours, sometimes late at night when I couldn't sleep anyway.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if grades had been released. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed an announcement because missing something important would spike my anxiety. I also logged in when I needed to watch lecture videos or check discussion boards, though I'll admit I didn't log in as frequently as I probably should have.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do. I'd sometimes forget about it for a few days and then have a mini panic when I remembered to check. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me be more intentional about when I engaged with it, but it still felt like effort rather than routine.
Q5: The video lectures were probably most useful because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content at my own pace. I also found the assignment submission portals helpful because they gave me clear structure and deadlines. Discussion posts were occasionally useful when classmates shared different perspectives, though I didn't engage with them as much as I could have.
Q6: I often ignored the supplementary readings and some of the optional resources. With 14 courses to juggle and my anxiety levels being what they are, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list. I know I probably missed out on deeper learning, but survival mode kicked in a lot.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to my grades or my understanding of core concepts. Visual materials like diagrams, flowcharts, or well-organized slides immediately felt valuable to me. If something was confusing or seemed disconnected from what I needed to know for assessments, I'd lose motivation to engage with it quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities had this weight of necessity that pushed through my resistance, but optional ones? My brain would immediately categorize them as 'nice to have' and deprioritize them. When I was less stressed, I'd sometimes explore optional content, but those moments were rare this semester.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded most of the lecture slides and PDFs so I could study them on my tablet without needing internet. I created extensive handwritten notes with diagrams and visual maps that I'd review constantly. I also used YouTube videos and external websites to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials alone.
Q10: So many situations. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams and creating visual summaries from downloaded materials without ever logging into the LMS. I'd study with classmates in person, quizzing each other and explaining concepts. Some of my best learning happened lying in bed reviewing my color-coded notes, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture videos or PDFs once and then work with them offline for days or even weeks. My attendance wasn't great because I found I could learn better by rewatching recorded content at my own pace. The LMS would show one download, but that material might have been the basis for ten hours of studying.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. My anxiety would spike so high that just opening the LMS felt overwhelming. I'd have moments where I was too stressed to function properly, and the mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through some of those times. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also made it harder to stay engaged consistently.
Q13: Hugely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Confusion about assignment requirements would sometimes paralyze me—I'd spend more time anxious about doing something wrong than actually doing the work. My other responsibilities meant I often had to choose between engaging deeply with course content or just getting things submitted.
Q14: Yes, definitely. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through materials without actually absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine learning, but I'd do it anyway hoping something would stick.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when my anxiety was high. Starting an assignment felt like this huge mountain to climb, so I'd avoid it even knowing the deadline was approaching. It's not that I didn't care—I cared too much, which made the fear of failing or doing poorly paralyzing. The mindfulness techniques helped me recognize this pattern and work through it somewhat.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before things were due, which probably looked chaotic from a data perspective. Without deadlines, I'd drift and procrastinate. The pressure of a deadline would cut through my anxiety and force me into action, even if it meant last-minute cramming sessions.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood and make me want to engage more, but critical feedback sometimes sent my anxiety spiraling. I'd obsess over what I did wrong rather than using it constructively. Over time, with the mindfulness practice, I got slightly better at processing feedback without catastrophizing.
Q18: The structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed given how scattered my natural tendencies are. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped me know what to focus on. But when the structure was unclear or when multiple courses had conflicting demands, I'd struggle to prioritize and my engagement would suffer.
Q19: Visual organization of the course made engagement much easier—when I could see a clear roadmap of what was expected, I felt more in control. What made it harder was when instructions were buried in long text blocks or when important information wasn't highlighted. My visual learning style meant I really needed things to be laid out clearly and accessibly.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things repeatedly without actually submitting work. Or they might be struggling to understand the material and keep revisiting it without making progress. For me, high activity sometimes meant I was panicking and refreshing pages obsessively rather than productively engaging.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was low and I didn't log in super frequently, but I still managed a Merit because I was doing so much work offline. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and external resources. Performance and visible activity aren't always correlated.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which gives some insight into behavior. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all offline studying, emotional states, and the real struggles students face that affect how they show up in the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm genuinely curious and making connections between concepts, even if I'm stressed. For me, engagement also means pushing through the anxiety and showing up even when it's hard—it's not just about being present, it's about being mentally invested.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought through anxiety to complete assignments, or the mindfulness exercises I did before studying to calm myself down. It wouldn't capture the tears of frustration or the pride I felt when I finally understood something difficult. It also wouldn't show the extensive offline work that actually formed the backbone of my learning this semester.
